field_notes.character: The kana character itself. Yōon entries (e.g., きゃ) are two-codepoint strings.
field_notes.script: Which script the entry belongs to: hiragana or katakana.
field_notes.romaji: Hepburn romanization. Doubled consonants for sokuon; double-vowel for long vowels (kōhī = ko-u-hi-i).
field_notes.romaji_alt: Alternative romanizations where Hepburn and Kunrei-shiki differ (e.g., shi vs si, fu vs hu, ja vs zya).
field_notes.type: Entry classification: base, dakuten, handakuten, yoon, yoon_dakuten, yoon_handakuten, sokuon, or other (archaic, long vowel mark).
field_notes.base: For derived forms (dakuten/handakuten/yōon), the base kana this is derived from.
field_notes.stroke_count: Standard stroke count. Null for yōon compound forms (because they are composed of two base kana, each with its own stroke count).
field_notes.unicode: Lowercase hex Unicode codepoint. For multi-codepoint forms (yōon), the codepoints are joined with '+'.
field_notes.usage_notes: Human-readable notes on when and how the entry is used. Present only where there is something non-obvious to say.

metadata.authorship_caveat: Stroke counts and romanizations are the author's best knowledge. Corrections welcome via docs/contributing.md and docs/upstream-issues.md.

kana.usage_notes: Yōon (palatalized) compound. Written as the base kana plus small ya/yu/yo.

Small tsu. Indicates a doubled consonant (gemination), written as っ before the consonant it doubles. Transcribed as a doubled consonant in romaji (e.g., きっぷ kippu).
Katakana small tsu. Same function as っ; gemination in loanwords and emphatic writing.
Archaic. Obsolete in modern Japanese (post-1946 orthography reform); replaced by い in most contexts. Still appears in historical texts and some proper names.
Archaic. Obsolete in modern Japanese (post-1946 orthography reform); replaced by え in most contexts. Still appears in historical texts.
Archaic. Obsolete counterpart to ゐ.
Archaic. Obsolete counterpart to ゑ. Occasionally seen in brand names (e.g., ヱビス beer).
Chōonpu (long vowel mark). Lengthens the preceding vowel in katakana (e.g., コーヒー kōhī). Typically only used in katakana; long vowels in hiragana are written by doubling or with an extra vowel kana.
